snippets.title: Create, index, unpack
snippets.code:
pt = (3, 4)
print(pt[0], pt[1])     # 3 4

x, y = pt               # unpack
first, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4)   # first=1, rest=[2,3,4]

singleton = (42,)        # trailing comma is required
not_a_tuple = (42)       # this is just an int — parentheses are grouping
snippets.explanation: The trailing comma — not parentheses — is what makes a tuple.

snippets.title: Immutability
snippets.code:
pt = (3, 4)
# pt[0] = 5            # TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment

new_pt = (5, pt[1])     # produce a new tuple instead
print(pt, new_pt)        # (3, 4) (5, 4)
snippets.explanation: You can't mutate a tuple in place; produce a new one.

snippets.title: Tuple as a cache / dict key
snippets.code:
cache: dict[tuple[str, int], str] = {}
key = ("gpt-4o-mini", hash("hello"))
cache[key] = "<response>"

print(cache[("gpt-4o-mini", hash("hello"))])
snippets.explanation: A tuple of hashables is hashable; ideal for compound cache keys.

snippets.title: Named tuple
snippets.code:
from collections import namedtuple

Usage = namedtuple("Usage", ["prompt", "completion", "total"])
u = Usage(120, 80, 200)
print(u.prompt, u.completion, u.total)   # field access
# u.prompt = 0   # AttributeError — still immutable
snippets.explanation: namedtuple gives field names without losing tuple semantics; a frozen dataclass is the modern alternative.
